caption_text:
Islands of the terminator.

Made by raw sunlight off crater peaks.  Citizens of the sublime.

Can you see them?

Paws your scrolling to enjoy some space right meow 🐈

This image of the Cat’s Paw Nebula is a cosmic collab between @NASAWebb and @NASAChandraXray. Chandra contributed X-ray data—the purple blotches within the dusty center of the nebula—to this image snapped by Webb in infrared.

X-rays are energetic beams of light that can break through thick gas and dust, which help scientists study the high-energy activity in the region.

Credit: X-ray: NASA/SAO/CXC; Infrared: NASA/ESA/CSA/STScI; Image Processing: NASA/CXC/SAO/J. Major

#NASA #JWST #Chandra #Cats

Every cloud has an… icy lining?⁣

In parts of our Milky Way galaxy, there are giant molecular clouds – vast regions of gas and dust where clumps of matter collapse under gravity, giving birth to stars. Our SPHEREx (Spectro-Photometer for the History of the Universe, Epoch of Reionization, and Ices Explorer) mission mapped ice found inside those clouds.⁣

One of the things SPHEREx does is map the chemical signatures of various types of interstellar ice. This ice includes molecules like water, carbon dioxide, and carbon monoxide, which are vital to the chemistry that allows life to develop. 

In this image, we can see the presence of water ice in bright blue. Researchers believe these ice reservoirs, attached to the surfaces of tiny dust grains, are where most of the universe’s water is formed and stored.⁣
 ⁣
Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech/IPAC/Hora et al.⁣
 ⁣
#NASA #Space #MilkyWay #Stars⁣
Last week, we took the mission to the heart of New York City. The Artemis II crew showed the country what’s next. America is ready to go back to the Moon. Artemis II is just the beginning. 🌕

caption_text:
Swipe across to see a rocket launch.
 
This wide shot of Launch Complex 39A at @nasakennedy shows a dramatic infrared black-and-white view of the rocket that carried our Crew-11 mission to the @ISS on August 1, 2025.
 
Crew-11 flew to the station as part of our Commercial Crew program, where we partner with private industry to send astronauts on science missions in low Earth orbit. Crew-13 is next in line in our series of missions, scheduled to launch no earlier than September 2026.
 
This photo was recognized in our 2025 Photographer of the Year awards. Check out the full set at go.nasa.gov/25photo or visit the link in our bio.
 
Credit: NASA/Joel Kowsky
 
#NASA #Launch #Rocket #Crew11

Start your week with some new photos from Artemis II!

Though our journey around the Moon has ended, we're still retrieving plenty of new images. Keep an eye on our Artemis II multimedia gallery for image highlights from the mission—the link is in our bio!
 
#NASA #Artemis #Photodump

A NASA photographer captured a cheerful farewell between NASA astronaut Chris Williams and his children ahead of his voyage to the International Space Station last fall.⁣

Williams launched aboard the Roscosmos Soyuz MS-28 spacecraft on Nov. 27, 2025, for an approximately eight-month mission aboard the @ISS. There, he's working on experiments that can benefit humans on Earth and in space as we return to the Moon and go on to Mars.⁣

This photo is one of the winners in the People category of our Photographer of the Year awards. Check out the full set: go.nasa.gov/25photo⁣

Credit: NASA/Bill Ingalls⁣

#NASA #Astronaut #Family

Today, @Nasdaq had the distinct honor of welcoming the @NASA Artemis II crew to the Nasdaq Closing Bell: Commander Reid Wiseman (@astro_reid), Pilot Victor Glover (@AstroVicGlover) and Mission Specialists Christina Koch (@Astro_Christina) and Jeremy Hansen (@AstroJeremy).

The Artemis II mission represents decades of innovation, thousands of brilliant minds collaborating across borders, and humanity’s commitment to pushing beyond what we thought possible.

Congratulations to @NASA and the entire Artemis II team.

"People often told us that we were making history coming to launch, and humbly I believe them. But moreover, we were making the future." 

@NASA Artemis II Mission Specialist @astro_christina takes the Nasdaq podium alongside fellow crew members, Commander @astro_reid, Pilot @astrovicglover and Mission Specialist @astrojeremy in celebration of their recent successful space mission.

caption_text:
POV: you’re rolling around on the Red Planet

You’re looking at six years on Mars in under two minutes. This timelapse contains images captured by our Curiosity rover between 2020 and 2026 from one of its navigation cameras. These photos, looking behind the rover, help our scientists choose Martian rocks to study.

Curiosity first arrived on Mars in 2012 after launching from Earth the year before. Since then, it’s been digging into the history of Mars, studying what the Red Planet looked like when it may have been home to microbial life.

Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

caption_text:
Sleep in space. It’s the best. 

I claimed an “upside down” vertical spot in the tallest part of the capsule, as the only one who fit. Why hang like a bat? For one, because it feels no different than any other direction! For the other reason, read on…

Did you know that air doesn’t naturally circulate in space? That’s because there’s no convection or other density-driven gas movement without gravity. So, space modules have well-designed ventilation to circulate carbon dioxide into the scrubbing system and provide fresh air to breathe.

The top docking hatch area of our capsule was analyzed to have not enough active ventilation to be safe to sleep in, so I had to be upside down and have my head near the floor of the main cabin. It definitely was a bit confusing when I first woke up!

I also learned that making a demo video in a cozy spaceship is a team effort!
